schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil weigh the ambiguous preposition — David's heart 'toward' or 'against' Absalom; the hostile sense makes Joab's stratagem necessary.
Matthew Henry anatomizes Joab's four motives: courtier, friend, statesman fearing civil war, and a fellow-murderer seeking precedent for mercy.
Gill fixes Joab as the general who read and worked upon the king's mood.
Wesley: David longed to see Absalom but was ashamed, and wanted the fair pretence Joab supplied.

debates.question: Was the king's heart 'toward' Absalom in affection or 'against' him in hostility?

sides.position: affection — David yearned for his son
hostility — the preposition elsewhere means 'against,' which alone makes Joab's ruse needful

schools.summary: Tekoah is located near Bethlehem (home of Amos); the woman is a respectable 'wise woman,' not a witch, chosen at a distance so the case could not be checked.
Clarke lists the circumstances — widow, distant, aged, mourning, a veiled parallel — all combined to move the king; Wesley notes the un-anointed mourning.
Gill notes only that the woman was to come before the king in her feigned mourning condition.
Keil stresses the closing clause: Joab framed both disguise and speech, so it is his craft that will move the king.

themes: Joab puts words in her mouth

schools.summary: A textual note: many witnesses read the woman 'came' to the king rather than 'spake'; Ellicott calls it immaterial, Keil defends the Masoretic reading.
Keil unfolds the fabricated calamity — a widow, two sons, a fratricide, and the family demanding the survivor — and defends the grammar of 'he smote him, the other.'
Clarke thinks the facts may have been partly real; Wesley notes the widow is one whom the king is bound by God's law especially to relieve.

The two sons are chosen to mirror Amnon and Absalom; Ellicott notes the fratricide is painted as unpremeditated — unlike Absalom's — precisely to win a favourable judgement before the difference is remembered.
Gill: the number 'two' is deliberate, that the case might suit Amnon and Absalom.
Clarke unpacks 'quench my coal' — a man's posterity is his lamp (2 Sam 21:17; Ps 132:17); to quench it is to extinguish the family, an image the heathen also used.
Ellicott sees a covert allusion to Absalom in 'destroy the heir also'; Keil judges the king's coming promise of protection perfectly just for an unpremeditated killing.
Gill: not the next of kin only but the whole kindred rise as one man demanding justice.
Keil defends the king's promise of protection as just on the facts presented.
Clarke: the promise was 'too distant' to conclude her business, so she presses on.
Gill takes the words as an invitation to go home and be easy under the king's care.
Ellicott: she prolongs the interview to make the king commit himself; Keil: she is crafty, working to bind him by an oath.
Clarke: she offers to bear all blame so the throne stays guiltless; Wesley: her words decently insinuate that neglect would bring guilt on the king.
Gill: she speaks with vehemence and respect, dreading scrutiny of a case that could not bear it.

themes: protecting the judge to hold him

schools.summary: Gill: the king now offers personal protection against any who reproach her or demand her son.
Clarke: even this fails her aim — a solemn promise to stop all proceedings against her son — so she presses further.
Ellicott: the crafty woman now leads the king to bind himself with the solemnity of an oath.
Clarke: God shows mercy promptly when entreated, and 'As the LORD liveth' is the very oath she meant to extort; Wesley: God is invoked as witness against the king if he break it.
Henry hears in 'not one hair fall' a shadow of the Son of David's promise that not a hair of his own shall perish (Luke 21:18).
Gill: the plea calls the king to imitate the merciful God, and perhaps to swear by him.
Gill: her point gained and sworn, she now applies the parable to Absalom, improving her case in his favour.
Keil: her language is deliberately indefinite, hinting that the king inculpates himself by not fetching home his banished one.
'The people of God' means Israel; the king wrongs the nation and contradicts his own judgement by leaving Absalom banished — 'as one guilty' by his own word.
Clarke and Wesley press the consistency: mercy granted a stranger's fratricide is denied the king's own son, whose death would grieve all Israel; David condemns himself.
Henry: though Absalom's case really differed, David was too fond to note it — 'change but the name, and the tale is told of thee.'

The difficult clause on the soul is variously read (Hebrew, LXX, Vulgate); Ellicott: God in wrath remembers mercy and 'devises means' for the banished, but the woman craftily shifts from justice to life's uncertainty.
Henry lifts the verse into a gospel: God's patience and his provision of atonement mean the sinner banished by sin need not be expelled for ever.
Wesley: all must die — king, Absalom, Amnon; the closing clause echoes the manslayer's return at the high priest's death.
Gill judges the argument, applied to sparing a real murderer, to be poor reasoning however plausible.

themes: God devises means for the banished
the state of sinners as banishment

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'the people have made me afraid' is studiedly ambiguous — the nation or her own kin — keeping both the fiction and the Absalom application alive at once.
Wesley reads a real political warning beneath the veil: the people, discontented at Absalom's banishment, might rise if he invaded.
Gill: she resumes the cover of her own feigned case.
Keil: 'the inheritance of God' is Israel, God's portion (1 Sam 26:19; Deut 32:9); to be cut off from it is to be driven from the people and worship of the LORD.
Wesley: the phrase presses Absalom's peril as an exile among idolaters, cut off from law and ordinances, while 'me and my son together' touches David in a tender place.
Gill: she is fully persuaded of the king's readiness to hear.
'As an angel of God' is a recognised idiom for more-than-human discernment (2 Sam 19:27; 1 Sam 29:9); Keil: the king listens to every just complaint, yet the very polish betrays Joab's hand.
Wesley: the king is angel-like in wisdom, justice, and goodness, and therefore God will stand by him in this act of grace.
Gill: 'comfortable' means 'for rest' — ease to her and to all Israel at the news of Absalom's recall.
Keil: the woman's words were so crafty the king saw both her meaning and that she had not come of her own accord, and so demands an undisguised answer.
Gill: David now plainly understood the feigned case and its intent — to convey the people's mind about Absalom.

themes: the plot brought into the open

schools.summary: Keil renders her frank confession: none can turn right or left from the king's word, for Joab bade her and put the words in her mouth.
Henry makes her candour a rule: she takes no praise to herself — 'if well done, let him have the thanks; if ill, let him bear the blame' — 'dare to be true; nothing can need a lie.'
Gill: the king inferred Joab from the very shrewdness of the scheme.
Gill: Joab framed the story to be 'transferred in a figure' to the king (1 Cor 4:6), disguising the real suit for Absalom.
Clarke: likening the king to an all-knowing angel is Asiatic court flattery, its excess a warning; Wesley: Joab's aim was to propose the people's desire parabolically.
A textual point: 'I have done' (a form of granting) versus the marginal 'thou hast done' (an implied censure of Joab); Keil infers Joab had pressed for the recall before.
Henry reads the reconciliation-through-intercession as a figure of the gospel: God reconciled to sinners only through a Mediator (2 Cor 5:19), Christ fetching the banished home.

debates.question: Is the reading 'I have done this thing' or 'thou hast done'?

sides.position: 'I have done' — the true text, simply granting Joab's request
'thou hast done' — the margin, carrying an implied censure of Joab

themes: a gospel type and its flaw

schools.summary: Keil: Joab's thanks show he had long and repeatedly sought Absalom's return, which is why David suspected his hand.
Henry: Joab took it as a kindness and perhaps a hint of impunity for Abner's murder — but was mistaken (1 Kings 2:5-6).
Wesley: David could not dispense with God's laws, which bind kings as well as people; how justly God made the spared Joab a scourge to him.
Gill and Keil supply the geography: Joab fetches Absalom from Geshur, his grandfather's kingdom (2 Sam 13:37), back to Jerusalem.
Henry protests the breach of Genesis 9:6 — God's laws are not cobwebs that let the great flies through; God made the spared Absalom a scourge.
Keil and Ellicott call the half-forgiveness an imprudent measure that bore bitter fruit — Absalom irritated yet well placed to plot, likely confined to his house.
Clarke and Wesley read the exclusion as showing detestation of the crime and aiming at Absalom's humiliation and repentance.
Henry: the interdict served the king's honour and his son's humbling; David doubted his penitence but would gladly restore him on true sorrow.
Keil notes the idiom 'to much praising'; Clarke: Absalom's beauty caught the people, who are led by outward appearance.
Henry: nothing is said of his wisdom or piety, only his looks — a polluted soul in a comely body; parents fonder of beauty than virtue have reason to fear.
Wesley: his beauty is the occasion of his pride and of the people's affection.

themes: comeliness no measure of a man

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott treat the 200 shekels as a round and excessive number, likely a copyist's error, the heavy hair a sign of vigour.
Clarke's long dissertation canvasses ancient authorities on shekel weights, oils and gold-dust, and proposes a miscopied numeral (thirty for two hundred).
Henry: the hair is a fop's ornament, not a Nazarite's; pride feels no burden, and this hair 'proved his halter' (2 Sam 18:9).

themes: a textual crux in the numeral
the ornament that became a halter

schools.summary: The unnamed sons likely died in infancy (hence Absalom's memorial pillar, 2 Sam 18:18); the daughter Tamar is named for his wronged sister, and by Ellicott his line ran on through her to Maachah (1 Kings 15:2).
Gill: the sons died young, which explains the pillar Absalom raised for want of an heir.
Keil: after two years' exclusion Absalom sought full pardon through Joab, and when twice refused burned Joab's barley field to force him to come.
Henry: through five years Absalom stayed unhumbled, longing to see the king's face not from love but to supplant him — 'this snake cannot sting again till warmed in his father's bosom.'

themes: two years without the king's face

schools.summary: Ellicott: Joab, feeling he had done enough and the prince still in disfavour, refused twice — perhaps expecting some sign of penitence first; Keil records the double refusal.
Henry: Joab recoiled from Absalom's pride, for proud men take every service as a debt and Absalom had not owned the earlier kindness.
Ellicott: the arson was perfectly successful but the act of a lawless, unscrupulous character.
Clarke: strange conduct, but it had its effect — Joab, who would not stir before, now bestirs himself and succeeds.
Henry: as spiteful as Samson's burning of the Philistines' corn (Judg 15:5); Absalom owed restitution (Exod 22:6) yet neither offered nor was asked it.
Gill: Joab came provoked at the arson and demanding its reason — exactly the end Absalom had aimed at.
Keil records the outcome: the arson succeeds where two summons failed, and Joab comes to complain.
Ellicott: Absalom makes no acknowledgment of wrong but finds half-reconciliation intolerable — punish or pardon; David's compliance was great weakness he later suffered for.
Henry: an insolent message that undervalues the recall, denies the crime, and defies justice, knowing David loved him too much to kill him.
Wesley: 'kill me' is calculated pathos to insinuate himself into his father's affections; Absalom now dares to stand on his own justification.
Ellicott: in kissing Absalom in full reconciliation David showed great weakness, for which he suffered severely; Keil and Gill record the sequence.
Henry and Wesley: David's fondness misread an insolent bow as respect — yet the father's kiss becomes an image of God, who bemoans returning Ephraim as 'a dear son' (Jer 31:20).

themes: a broken image of divine mercy
